title: Using rainwater to flush toilets
subtitle: How gravity-fed and pumped systems work, and whether the payback stacks up
excerpt: Toilet flushing accounts for 30 percent of household water use. Replacing mains water with harvested rainwater for flushing can save 40 to 50 litres per person per day. A full gravity-fed system requires a tank, a pump, and a separate pipe run, which is significant work in an existing house. The simpler DIY option uses an elevated outside tank to gravity-feed a ground-floor toilet through an existing waste pipe or overflow route.
sourceNotes: CIRIA C539 Rainwater and Greywater Use in Buildings (2001). WRAS guidance on non-potable water systems in dwellings. EA Non-potable water use regulatory guidance (2023). Marsh Industries rainwater harvesting system design notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Water that is not safe or not approved for drinking, cooking, or food preparation. Harvested rainwater may be used for non-potable purposes including toilet flushing, garden irrigation, and washing machines. It must not be connected to the mains supply and must be clearly labelled to prevent accidental consumption.

content.text: Rainwater for toilet flushing is technically straightforward but financially marginal in the UK. Tap water costs around 1.50 to 2.00 pounds per cubic metre. A household of four saves about 50 to 60 m³ per year by flushing with rainwater. The saving is 75 to 120 pounds per year. A full pumped system costs 2,000 to 4,000 pounds installed. Payback takes 20 to 40 years, which exceeds the system life.
circuit in a new build costs 800 to 1,200 pounds rather than 2,000 to 4,000 pounds in retrofit. The payback is still long but more realistic.
Areas with metered water supply and high tariffs: in parts of the UK with higher water charges, the payback improves significantly.
Combined with irrigation: if a large tank is already in place for the garden, adding a toilet feed to it improves the payback.
Rainwater from the roof collects in a below-ground or above-ground tank via a leaf filter.
A submersible pump pressurises the rainwater circuit.
A separate pipe run (visually distinct from mains pipes, typically purple) feeds the toilet cisterns.
A mains backup float valve in the tank tops up automatically if the rainwater level falls below the pump minimum.

A large (1,000 to 2,000 litre) tank elevated at the top of an outbuilding, connected to a ground-floor toilet via a short pipe run, avoids the pump. This is only feasible when the toilet is on the ground floor and the tank can be sited high enough (2 m minimum above the toilet cistern fill valve). The pressure from 2 m of head (0.2 bar) is just enough for a float valve to function. This is a rare configuration and site-specific.

Connecting rainwater to the internal plumbing requires notification to the water company under the Water Supply Regulations 1999. The rainwater circuit must not connect to the mains supply. All non-potable pipework must be clearly labelled at every point.
